questions.text: Which number comes next in the series: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, __?
If all Roses are Flowers and some Flowers are Red, which conclusion is definitely true?
opts: All roses are red
Some roses may be red
No roses are red
All flowers are roses

questions.text: A train travels 240 km in 3 hours. At the same speed, how far will it travel in 5 hours?
opts: 350 km
400 km
380 km
420 km

questions.text: Find the odd one out: Triangle, Square, Circle, Sphere, Rectangle
opts: Triangle
Circle
Sphere
Rectangle

questions.text: If PENCIL is coded as PENCIL, how is ERASER coded in the same logic?
opts: REASRE
ERASER
RASEER
ARERES

questions.text: A clock shows 3:15. What is the angle between the hour and minute hands?
Which of these does NOT belong: 8, 27, 64, 100, 125?
Rearrange TNEELENCXLE to form a meaningful word:
opts: EXCELLENCE
EXELLENCTE
EXCELTENLE
NELLECTEXE

questions.text: A shopkeeper marks a product 40% above cost and gives a 20% discount. What is the profit %?

questions.context: A data table shows: City A population 2.4M grows at 3%/yr; City B population 3.1M grows at 1%/yr.
questions.text: In approximately how many years will City A's population exceed City B's?
opts: 12 years
15 years
18 years
21 years

questions.text: If the sum of 5 consecutive integers is 265, what is the middle integer?
A cube is painted red on all faces and cut into 125 smaller equal cubes. How many cubes have exactly 2 red faces?
Water flows into a tank at 15 L/min and drains at 8 L/min. Starting empty, how long to fill a 490 L tank?
opts: 60 min
65 min
70 min
75 min

questions.text: If 6 workers complete a task in 8 days, how many workers are needed to finish it in 3 days?
Three pipes A, B, C can fill a cistern in 6, 9, 12 hours respectively. All three are opened together. What fraction is filled in 1 hour?
A function f(x) = x³ − 6x² + 9x − 4. At which value of x does f(x) have a local minimum?

questions.context: A startup allocates ₹18L budget. Marketing:Development:Operations = 2:5:3. Development costs increase 20% mid-year.
questions.text: By how much must other departments reduce to keep the total budget unchanged?
opts: ₹1.8L total
₹2.0L total
₹2.16L total
₹1.5L total

questions.text: In a network of 7 cities, each pair connected by exactly one road, how many roads exist in total?
A sequence is defined by a₁=2, aₙ=3aₙ₋₁−1. What is a₅?
A wire of resistance 12Ω is bent into a circle. What is the resistance between two diametrically opposite points?
opts: 3Ω
4Ω
6Ω

questions.text: After 3 complete iterations, how many items remain?
questions.context: An algorithm processes N items. Each iteration doubles remaining items and removes 1. Starting at N=100.

questions.text: P, Q, R invest ₹5000, ₹6000, ₹4000. P withdraws half after 4 months. Total profit after 12 months is ₹6900. P's share?
A convex polygon has 20 diagonals. How many sides does it have?
I enjoy meeting new people and feel energised after social interactions.
I prefer to have a detailed plan before starting any task rather than figuring it out as I go.
I often think about abstract ideas, theories, and hypothetical scenarios for fun.
I find it easy to understand and share the feelings of others.
I stay calm and focused even when facing unexpected problems or setbacks.
I like to be in charge and naturally take the lead in group situations.
I find routine and predictability comforting rather than boring.
When I start something, I almost always follow through to completion.
Rate how accurately each statement describes you across these traits:
I enjoy helping others even when there is nothing in it for me.
I get anxious or overwhelmed when there is too much to do at once.
I prefer facts and evidence over intuition when making important decisions.
I enjoy solving puzzles, riddles, or problems that require logical thinking.
I express my thoughts and feelings better through writing, art, or speaking than through numbers.
I am willing to take risks and try new things, even if I might fail.
I keep working on a difficult task until I find a solution, even if it takes a long time.
I am curious about how things work and often ask questions to understand them better.
I work better alone than in a group when I need to focus on something important.
Rate how accurately each statement describes you:
rows: I notice when others are sad or upset
I enjoy debating and convincing others of my views
I am organised with my time and belongings
I get bored easily with repetitive tasks

questions.text: I feel comfortable making decisions quickly under pressure.
Which activity would you most enjoy doing for a full week?
opts: Coding an app or website
Designing a logo or illustration
Running a small business or startup
Conducting a science experiment
Writing a speech or news article
Organising a community event

questions.text: Which of these problems excites you most to solve?
opts: Why is traffic so bad in cities?
How do vaccines work at the molecular level?
Why do some businesses succeed while others fail?
How can AI understand human language?
How do bridges stay standing under load?
How do we reduce poverty in rural India?

questions.text: I would enjoy a career that involves working outdoors or with my hands.
If you had to pick a college course right now, which would you choose?
opts: Computer Science or Data Science
Medicine or Biotechnology
Architecture or Product Design
Law or Political Science
Commerce or Finance
Art, Music or Film

questions.text: Which type of book or content do you enjoy most in your free time?
opts: Tech, coding or science blogs
Biographies of leaders or history books
Business, economics or finance content
Mystery, crime or detective stories
Creative writing, poetry or art
Sports, fitness or nature documentaries

questions.text: I enjoy work that involves analysing data and finding patterns.
Which of these best describes your dream workplace?
opts: A research lab or tech company
A hospital or healthcare facility
A courtroom, government office or NGO
A design studio or creative agency
A bank, consultancy or startup
An outdoor field site or construction project

questions.text: I find artistic and creative activities more satisfying than technical ones.
Rate how much you enjoy each of these school subjects:
Which of these YouTube channels or shows would you watch on a free Sunday?
opts: Science explainer channels (Veritasium, Vsauce)
Medical or health documentaries
Business case studies (Shark Tank, startup stories)
History, politics or current affairs analysis
Art, design or film-making tutorials
Coding or technology product reviews

questions.text: In a school project, which role would you naturally pick?
opts: The researcher who finds and analyses information
The designer who makes it look attractive
The leader who organises everyone and plans timelines
The presenter who speaks in front of the class
The builder who creates the model or prototype
The writer who prepares the report

questions.text: I am curious about how the human body works and how diseases are treated.
I enjoy following news about the stock market, businesses, and the economy.
I enjoy debating social issues, politics, or arguing different sides of a topic.
Which of these activities sounds most fun to you?
opts: Building a working robot or machine
Solving a difficult math or physics problem
Helping a sick person feel better
Starting a small business and selling something
Writing a story, song or making a film
Organising a debate or social campaign

questions.text: I would enjoy a job where I get to invent new technology or machines.
Rate how interested you would be in each of these career activities:
rows: Writing computer programs or designing software
Reporting news or doing legal work

questions.text: Which of these famous people's careers would you most like to have?
opts: A doctor or medical researcher (like APJ Abdul Kalam, Dr. Devi Shetty)
A scientist or engineer (like CV Raman, Sundar Pichai)
A business leader (like Ratan Tata, Falguni Nayar)
A lawyer, judge or civil servant (like Fali Nariman, IAS officers)
An artist, writer or filmmaker (like Ruskin Bond, AR Rahman)
A social worker or activist (like Medha Patkar, Sonam Wangchuk)

questions.text: I prefer working with numbers, formulas and calculations over words and stories.
If you had ₹10,000 and one free month, what would you most enjoy doing?
opts: Building a small electronics or coding project
Volunteering at a hospital or clinic
Trading in stocks or starting a small online shop
Travelling and writing a blog or making a documentary
Attending a workshop on art, music or design
Running a campaign or awareness drive on a social issue

questions.context: Your close friend just failed an important exam they had studied very hard for. They are not saying much but look upset.
questions.text: What is your most natural response?
opts: Give them space — they'll talk when ready
Immediately try to cheer them up with jokes
Sit with them quietly and let them know you're there
Suggest practical steps to improve next time

questions.text: I notice when someone is feeling upset or uncomfortable, even when they don't say anything.

questions.context: You are working on a group project and one member is not contributing. The deadline is tomorrow.
questions.text: How do you handle it?
opts: Do their work quietly to avoid conflict
Confront them directly and firmly
Talk to them privately to understand why
Tell the group leader and let them deal with it

questions.text: When I make a mistake, I take responsibility for it rather than making excuses.
I can stay focused and perform well even when I am nervous or under pressure.

questions.context: You believe strongly that a decision being made by your group is wrong. The rest of the group disagrees.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Go along with the group to avoid friction
Express your view clearly but respect the group's final call
Try to change everyone's mind persistently
Leave the group if they won't change their decision

questions.text: I can identify what emotion I am feeling and understand why I feel that way.
I find it difficult to stay motivated when working on long tasks that take weeks or months.

questions.context: Your younger sibling accidentally breaks something of yours that mattered a lot to you. They look scared and immediately apologise.
questions.text: What is your most natural first reaction?
opts: Shout at them — they need to learn to be careful
Stay quiet, walk away, and process your anger alone first
Tell them it's okay even though you feel hurt inside
Acknowledge that you are upset but reassure them it was an accident

questions.text: When someone gives me harsh criticism about my work, I can listen calmly and find what is useful in it.

questions.context: You worked very hard for a competition but lost to someone you feel did not deserve to win as much as you did.
questions.text: How do you handle this feeling?
opts: Avoid the winner and complain about the result to others
Accept it outwardly but stay bitter for a long time
Congratulate the winner genuinely and analyse where you can improve
Tell yourself the competition was unfair and move on

questions.text: When my classmates do better than me in exams, I feel inspired rather than jealous.
I can usually tell what someone is feeling just by looking at their face or body language.

questions.context: Two of your close friends have had a big fight and both are now expecting you to take their side.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Pick the side of whoever you think is right
Avoid both of them until they sort it out themselves
Listen to both separately without taking sides and encourage them to talk
Tell them both they are being childish and to grow up

questions.text: Before reacting in anger, I am able to pause and think about what I want to say.

questions.context: Your parents are pressuring you to choose a career path that you do not feel passionate about. They have strong opinions and get upset when you push back.
questions.text: How do you handle the situation?
opts: Agree with them to keep peace, even though you feel unhappy inside
Argue back firmly until they understand your view
Listen to their concerns, share yours calmly, and ask for time to discuss
Avoid the conversation completely and decide silently on your own

questions.text: When I make a mistake, I am kind to myself instead of being harsh and self-critical.

questions.context: A new student joins your class who comes from a different region, speaks differently, and is being quietly mocked by some classmates.
questions.text: What is your most likely action?
opts: Stay out of it — it's not your problem
Quietly go and talk to the new student, making them feel welcome
Confront the classmates who are mocking them
Tell a teacher about what is happening

questions.text: Rate how often each statement is true for you:
rows: I can calm myself down when I am very upset
I bounce back quickly after a disappointment
I can express my feelings clearly in words
I notice when my mood is affecting how I treat others
I genuinely feel happy when others succeed

questions.context: You receive a very low grade on an assignment you genuinely thought you had done well on. You feel embarrassed and discouraged.
questions.text: What is your most likely response after the initial reaction?
opts: Decide the subject is not for you and lose interest
Blame the teacher for being unfair and tell your friends
Reflect on what went wrong and ask the teacher for feedback
Push the feeling aside and pretend it does not matter

questions.text: Which of these would make you feel most proud of your career?
opts: Inventing or building something used by millions
Earning enough to give your family a great life
Being recognised as the best in your field
Making a positive difference in people's daily lives
Having the freedom to create whatever you want
Holding a position of authority and responsibility

questions.text: Having financial security is more important to me than doing work I am passionate about.
I want my work to have a clear positive impact on society — even if it pays less.
You receive two job offers. One pays ₹1.5L/month doing work you find dull. The other pays ₹60K doing work you love. Which do you choose?
opts: ₹1.5L — financial security comes first
₹60K — passion matters more than money
It depends on other factors
I would negotiate both offers

questions.text: I enjoy recognition and being known as someone who excels in their field.
I would rather work in a stable government job than a risky but exciting startup.
Which value most guides your decisions in life?
opts: Integrity — always doing what is right
Achievement — always performing at my best
Compassion — always helping others first
Freedom — always having the choice to choose my own path

questions.text: I believe the meaning and purpose of my work matters more than anything else.
I would rather have a job that gives me time for family and hobbies than one that pays much more but takes up all my time.
Which of these would matter most to you in your future career?
opts: Learning new things and growing every year
Earning enough to support my family well
Being respected and looked up to in society
Having freedom to make my own decisions
Building deep relationships with my colleagues
Competing and being the best at what I do

questions.text: Earning the respect and admiration of my family and community matters a lot to me.
I would happily take a lower salary if my work involves continuously learning and growing.
Which work setting would suit you best in the long run?
opts: Working independently — being my own boss
Working in a small close-knit team
Working in a large organisation with clear structure
Working directly with clients or the public
Working in a research or creative environment
Working in a leadership role managing others

questions.text: I am willing to work very hard for many years now if it leads to a much better life later.

questions.context: You are offered a very high-paying job, but to do well in it you would need to occasionally bend the rules and not be fully honest with clients.
questions.text: What would you do?
opts: Accept it — that is how the real world works
Accept it but try to be honest wherever possible
Refuse it — honesty is non-negotiable for me
Take it for a few years, save money, then leave

questions.text: I prefer doing things in a tried and tested way rather than constantly trying new methods.
Where would you most like to live and work in your future?
opts: In my hometown, close to family
In a major Indian city like Bengaluru, Mumbai or Delhi
Abroad — in the US, UK, Europe or similar
In a small town or rural area helping local communities
Travelling frequently — different places, no fixed base
Wherever the best opportunity takes me

questions.text: I am motivated more by winning and being the best than by working together with others as a team.

questions.context: Imagine you are 30 years old. You have to choose between two life paths.
questions.text: Which one would you most likely pick?
opts: A senior role at an established company with good salary, predictable life and weekends off
Founding your own startup — long hours, uncertain income, but full ownership of what you build
A government or teaching job with strong job security and a respected place in society
A creative or social-impact career with modest income but deep personal satisfaction

questions.text: Rate how important each of these is to you in choosing a career:
rows: Helping people and making a difference

questions.context: You are selected as team leader for a school science fair project. Two team members have a strong disagreement about the project direction and neither will back down.
questions.text: As leader, what do you do first?
opts: Decide yourself and ask everyone to follow
Let them argue it out — the better idea will win
Facilitate a structured discussion where both sides present their case
Ask a teacher to resolve it

questions.context: You discover that a classmate copied answers from you in an exam without your knowledge. The teacher suspects you were both cheating.
questions.text: How do you respond?
opts: Deny everything — you didn't do anything wrong
Tell the teacher exactly what happened honestly
Protect your classmate — it's not a big deal
Stay quiet and hope it blows over

questions.context: You are midway through a 3-month project when you realise the approach you've been using has a fundamental flaw. Starting over will cost weeks.
questions.text: What is your most likely action?
opts: Keep going — too much work already done
Immediately restart with the correct approach
Find a workaround to patch the flaw without restarting
Discuss with your mentor or team before deciding

questions.context: Your manager assigns you a task with unclear instructions. When you ask for clarification, they say "just figure it out."
questions.text: How do you proceed?
opts: Do nothing until you get clear instructions
Make your best interpretation, document it, and proceed
Ask a colleague who might know what's expected
Escalate to someone more senior

questions.context: You work at a company and notice a senior colleague regularly taking credit for a junior team member's ideas in meetings.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: It's not your business — stay out of it
Speak privately to the junior colleague and encourage them to speak up
Raise it directly with the senior colleague
Mention it to a manager or HR

questions.context: You have been given two weeks to complete a major assignment. After one week you realise you have significantly underestimated the work needed.
questions.text: What is your first step?
opts: Work day and night to somehow finish on time
Immediately communicate the situation to whoever assigned the task
Submit whatever you have on the deadline
Ask for an extension without explaining why

questions.context: You strongly believe a decision your school or organisation is making is ethically wrong, but you are the most junior person in the room.
questions.text: What would you do?
opts: Stay silent — it's not your place
Wait and see if anyone else raises it first
Speak up respectfully and clearly explain your concern
Write a private letter to the decision-maker

questions.context: You are presenting a project to an audience when someone asks a technical question you genuinely do not know the answer to.
questions.text: How do you respond?
opts: Bluff — give a confident-sounding answer
Say honestly you don't know and will follow up
Change the subject smoothly
Ask the audience if anyone knows

questions.context: You have an exam tomorrow, an assignment due today, and a close friend has just called saying they urgently need to talk about a personal problem.
questions.text: What is your most likely course of action?
opts: Tell your friend you'll call back tomorrow — exams come first
Drop everything and meet your friend immediately
Talk to your friend briefly to understand urgency, then plan accordingly
Submit the assignment quickly, then call your friend, then study

questions.context: You give a class presentation and forget your lines midway. Some students laugh and you feel humiliated.
questions.text: What do you do next?
opts: Quickly end the presentation and rush back to your seat
Pause, take a breath, acknowledge the slip lightly and continue
Make an excuse like feeling unwell and stop
Push through stiffly without acknowledging anything

questions.context: Your friends are planning to skip school to watch a movie and are pressuring you to come along. You don't want to go but they call you boring for refusing.
questions.text: How do you respond?
opts: Go along with them — friends matter more than one day of school
Refuse firmly without making a big deal about it
Make up an excuse like being unwell to get out of it
Refuse and try to convince them not to go either

questions.context: You notice that the school computer lab's printer has been broken for over a week, affecting many students. No one has fixed it and no teacher seems to be acting on it.
questions.text: What is your most likely action?
opts: Wait — someone in charge will eventually handle it
Complain about it with classmates but do nothing
Approach a teacher or admin staff and report it clearly
Try to fix it yourself or find someone who can

questions.context: You want to start a small initiative at school — perhaps a coding club or a charity drive — but you need permission from a teacher who is generally hesitant about new ideas.
questions.text: How do you approach this?
opts: Drop the idea — it isn't worth the trouble
Approach the teacher with a clear plan, written proposal and how it benefits students
Get a group of friends and approach the teacher together to add weight
Go to a different teacher who might be more open to the idea

questions.context: A teacher has unfairly accused you of something you did not do in front of the whole class. Other students saw you didn't do it but stayed silent.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Stay quiet — challenging a teacher publicly will only make it worse
Defend yourself politely in class right then and there
Speak to the teacher privately after class to clear the matter
Tell your parents and ask them to handle it

questions.context: You have been given a school project to build a working model but the budget you have is very small and the materials you originally planned for are too expensive.
questions.text: What is your most likely approach?
opts: Submit a simpler model that fits the budget
Ask parents for extra money to buy the original materials
Look for cheaper alternatives or recycled materials that still work
Team up with classmates to share costs and resources

questions.context: You are struggling badly with one school subject. A classmate offers to help you regularly, but you feel embarrassed accepting help.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Politely refuse and try to figure it out alone
Accept the help gratefully and offer to help them in another subject in return
Accept the help just once to see if it works
Ask a teacher or tutor instead — it feels less embarrassing

questions.context: You find a wallet with ₹2000 cash and an ID card on the school playground. There are no witnesses around.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Hand over the entire wallet to the school office or security
Use the ID to find the owner and return it directly
Take it home and figure out what to do later
Keep the cash since no one saw and hand in just the wallet

questions.context: Your best friend tells you a serious personal secret — they are being bullied online and ask you not to tell anyone, including their parents.
questions.text: What do you do?
opts: Respect their wish and stay silent as promised
Stay silent but try to support them and reduce the bullying yourself
Tell a trusted adult quietly even though you promised not to
Convince your friend to tell their parents or a counsellor themselves

questions.context: You receive ₹5000 as a gift on your birthday. You want a new gadget worth ₹4500 but you also know your savings are very low.
questions.text: What is your most likely decision?
opts: Buy the gadget — birthday money is meant to be enjoyed
Save all of it for the future
Save most of it and spend a small portion on something small you like
Buy the gadget but only after saving enough on your own to cover most of it

questions.context: You and a classmate worked equally hard on a group project, but the teacher praises only the classmate by name in front of the class. You feel hurt and overlooked.
questions.text: How do you handle it?
opts: Stay quiet and feel bitter about it for a while
Talk to the teacher privately and respectfully share that you also contributed
Confront the classmate and ask them to clarify with the teacher
Let it go — recognition isn't the most important thing
